patterns.title: Pattern Problems
patterns.tagline: Solve any pattern in seconds — see the formula before you finish reading the problem.

tricks.title: The 3-second decode
tricks.summary: When you see a pattern, ask only three questions in order.
tricks.html:
When a pattern shows up, ask only these three questions in your head:

  1. How many rows? (this is your outer loop length)
  2. What's the leftmost thing on row i? (spaces? content? what character?)
  3. How long is row i and where does it stop?

If you can answer those three in under 5 seconds, you've already solved it — coding is just typing.

tricks.title: The 5 atomic shapes
tricks.summary: Every pattern is one of these — or a combo. Train your eye to recognize, not solve.
tricks.html:
ShapeWhat you seeMental cue
BlockFilled rectangle“every row identical”
SlopeGrowing or shrinking triangle“row i has i (or N−i+1)”
ConeCentered pyramid“spaces before, odd-count content after”
MirrorVertically symmetric“build top half, copy-paste flipped”
RingHollow / concentric“border only, or distance-from-edge”

When you see Pattern 9 (Diamond) — your brain should fire: “Cone + Cone-flipped”. Pattern 19 → “Cone going outside-in, mirrored”. Pattern 17 → “Cone with letters that climb-and-descend”.

You're not solving from scratch — you're combining 5 shapes you already know.

tricks.title: The 4 magic formulas
tricks.summary: Memorize once. They cover ~70% of all 22 patterns.
tricks.html:
NeedFormula
Stars/numbers in row i (growing triangle)i
Stars in row i (shrinking triangle)N − i + 1
Stars in centered pyramid (odd count)2i − 1
Leading spaces in centered pyramidN − i

Letter version: the j-th letter (0-indexed) is (char)('A' + j). Patterns 14–18 are stars-patterns with (char)('A'+j) instead of *.

tricks.title: Imagine the pen — the universal template
tricks.summary: Pretend you're drawing top-to-bottom, left-to-right. For every row, just two zones.
tricks.html:
Pretend you're drawing the pattern with a single pen, top to bottom, left to right. For every row:

  • How many steps with the pen UP (skipping = spaces)?
  • How many steps with the pen DOWN (drawing = stars/letters)?
  • Drop the pen, hit Enter, next row.

This collapses every pattern into the same template:

for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
    skip( leadingSpaces(i) );
    draw( contentCount(i), whatChar(i, j) );
    println();
}

You're solving the same problem 22 times — only changing 3 small functions.

tricks.title: The 22-pattern memory journey (story chain)
tricks.summary: One linked story you walk through — each scene cues the next pattern.
tricks.html: You walk up to a brick wall. You climb stairs going up — each step has numbers 1, 12, 123 painted on it. Each floor shouts its own number — “1!”, “22!”, “333!” Reaching the top, you turn around and walk back down the same stairs, counting backwards. At the bottom you find a Christmas tree — you flip it upside-down, then put it on top of the original to make a diamond ring. You slice the diamond in half. Inside the half-diamond is a panel of binary lights flickering 0, 1, 0, 1. The lights crown a king wearing a mirrored number crown. The king is counting continuously, never resetting — 1, 23, 456, 78910. He climbs alphabet stairs (A, AB, ABC), slides back down them, and each step echoes its letter (A, BB, CCC). On the last step is a pyramid of chanting letters — ABCBA. Behind the pyramid, a kid is sliding down a slide that starts later every time — E, DE, CDE. The slide ends in a giant hourglass sealed inside a butterfly's wings, framed by a picture frame, hung on a wall made of onion rings.

tricks.title: All 22 patterns — hook + formula table
tricks.summary: Visual hook for each pattern + the one-line formula.
tricks.html:
#Hook imageShapeFormula
1🧱 Brick wallfull squareN stars × N rows
2📈 Stairs going upgrowing trianglerow i = i stars
3🪜 Numbered stairs1, 12, 123print j for j=1..i
4📢 Floor shouts itself1, 22, 333print i, i times
5📉 Stairs going downshrinking trianglerow i = N-i+1 stars
6🔢 Backwards numbered12345, 1234print j for j=1..N-i+1
7🎄 Christmas treecentered pyramid(N-i) spaces + (2i-1) stars
8🙃 Upside-down treeinverted pyramid(i-1) spaces + (2(N-i)+1) stars
9💎 Diamond ringtree + flipped treeP7 then P8
10🔺🔻 Sliced diamondhalf diamondstairs up then stairs down
11💡 Binary lights0/1 alternatingstart = i%2; flip with ^=1
12👑 Number crown12 211..i + spaces(2(N-i)) + i..1
13🏃 Continuous counter1, 23, 456int k=1; print k++ in inner
14🔤 Alphabet stairsA, AB, ABCprint 'A'+j for j=0..i-1
15🔡 Alphabet stairs downABCDE, ABCDprint 'A'+j for j=0..N-i
16🪞 Letter echoA, BB, CCCch = 'A'+i-1; print ch i times
17🗣️ Chanting pyramidABCBA(N-i) spaces; ch climbs to mid then descends
18🛝 Late-start slideE, DE, CDEprint 'A'+j for j=N-i..N-1
19⏳ Hourglasstop wide, narrow middle, mirrorstars + spaces = 2N (always)
20🦋 Butterflywings spread wide in middlei stars + 2(N-i) spaces + i stars; mirror
21🖼️ Picture framehollow rectangleif (border) '*' else ' '
22🧅 Onion ringsconcentric numbersvalue = N - min(i,j,W-1-i,W-1-j)

tricks.title: Family clusters — chunk 22 into 6 groups
tricks.summary: Memorize in mini-groups instead of 22 individuals.
tricks.html:
🟦 Group A — “Wall & Triangles” (1, 2, 5)

Wall = full, triangle up = step grows, triangle down = step shrinks.
One thought: blocks vs slopes.

🔢 Group B — “Numbers do tricks” (3, 4, 6)

Same shapes as A, but digits replace stars. 3 = column number, 4 = row number, 6 = column number on shrinking triangle.
One thought: what does each digit print — i or j?

🎄 Group C — “Trees & Diamonds” (7, 8, 9, 10)

Tree, flipped tree, both stacked = diamond, sliced = half.
One thought: centered = spaces before stars; odd star count.

💡 Group D — “Number tricks” (11, 12, 13)

Lights flicker (binary), crown is mirrored, counter never resets.
One thought: patterns about the digits themselves, not the shape.

🔤 Group E — “Alphabet show” (14–18)

Same shapes as A/B/C but with 'A' + j. Pattern 17 is the only twist (climb-and-descend).
One thought: swap stars for (char)('A' + something).

🪞 Group F — “Symmetric finale” (19–22)

Hourglass, butterfly, frame, onion. All symmetric.
One thought: width is fixed, content shifts in/out.

tricks.title: Cracking the tricky ones
tricks.summary: Patterns 4, 17, 19, 22 — what trips most people up.
tricks.html:
Pattern 17 (ABCBA) — “Climb then fall”

Track ONE variable ch = 'A'. Walk it up to the middle column, then walk it down. Same code, just ch++ becomes ch-- after midpoint.

for j = 1..(2i-1):
    print ch
    if j < mid → ch++   else → ch--

Pattern 19 (hourglass) — “Stars + spaces = 2N, always”

Every row has the same total width = 2N. The split is stars | spaces | stars. As i grows, stars shrink and spaces grow — they always sum to 2N.

Pattern 22 (concentric numbers) — “Distance from edge”

Stop thinking row-by-row. For every cell (i, j) in a (2N−1) square:

value = N − min(i, j, width-1-i, width-1-j)

One formula per cell. The four args are distances from top, left, bottom, right.

Pattern 4 (1, 22, 333) — “Print the row index, not the column”

Read the first column of expected output: if it changes per row → print i. If it's always 1 → print j. That's how you tell P4 from P3.

tricks.title: The 5-row sketch routine
tricks.summary: Do this 30-second drill for every new pattern. After 10 patterns it becomes reflex.
tricks.html:
For any unfamiliar pattern, write this table on paper:

row ispacescontent
1____
2____
3____
4____
5____

Fill it in by counting. Now look at column 2 — what's the formula? Now column 3 — what's the formula? You just derived f(i) and g(i). Code is mechanical from there.

tricks.title: Daily 10-minute drill
tricks.summary: The single practice routine that builds reflex in a week.
tricks.html:
1. Pick a random pattern (1–22).
  2. Cover the code. Look at output.
  3. Out loud, say: “f(i) = ___, g(i) = ___”.
  4. Open editor. Write it. Submit.
  5. If wrong, redo from step 3 — don't peek at code.

By day 3, you'll start seeing the formula before you finish reading the problem.

tricks.title: The mindset shift
tricks.summary: Stop naming patterns by their number. Start naming them by their shape.
tricks.html:
Stop saying “this looks like Pattern 17 from Striver” and start saying “this is a Cone with letters that climb-and-descend” or “this is a Slope counting from N down”.

Patterns aren't 22 things to memorize — they're 5 shapes × 3 contents (stars/numbers/letters) × 2 directions (up/down). Once your brain stops naming them and starts seeing them as combinations, you'll write any new pattern in under 60 seconds.
